output_text:
Strengthened the section by leading with the headline result, tightening claims, removing hedges, and sharpening the framing of evidence.

---

LLM-as-judge evaluators of text-to-SQL systems are biased toward verbose SQL, and that bias is currently scrambling published leaderboards. We introduce **QuerySieve**, a single-regression debiasing protocol that removes the length confound and recovers a length-invariant ranking — moving the Spearman correlation between judge scores and execution-match accuracy from 0.71 to 0.86 on SQL-Eval, and reordering the top of the board.

The problem is structural, not incidental. Benchmarks such as Spider 2.0 and BIRD have shifted from execution-based scoring to LLM judges because execution comparison is brittle under non-deterministic ordering and NULL handling. But these judges systematically prefer longer, more verbose SQL: given a reference query and two semantically equivalent candidates that return identical row sets, they reliably favor a multi-CTE rewrite over an inline-subquery formulation. Length, not correctness, is doing much of the work in current rankings.

QuerySieve corrects this with a generalized linear model in which the judge's pairwise preference probability is regressed on (i) system identity, (ii) reference-query token length, and (iii) candidate-query token length. The fitted system-identity coefficients are the debiased scores. Section 3 reports the full SQL-Eval results: correlation with execution-match accuracy improves from 0.71 to 0.86, and three systems previously ranked above CodeS-15B fall below it once length is partialled out — including systems whose lead over CodeS-15B had been treated as established.

The effect is not an artifact of one judge. GPT-4o-judge and Claude-3.5-judge yield system-identity coefficients within 0.03 of each other after length adjustment, despite differing markedly before it. This convergence is the signature we would expect if length bias is a property of the evaluation setup — the pairwise prompt, the rubric, the verbosity cues judges latch onto — rather than of any particular judge model. It also means the correction generalizes: practitioners need not commit to a specific judge to benefit.

The protocol is cheap and drop-in. It requires one regression fit per leaderboard run, with overhead negligible compared to the judging step itself. Any leaderboard that exposes pairwise judge preference probabilities can adopt it without modifying its underlying systems or its choice of judge.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I preserved every empirical claim and number from the original. The framing changes (e.g., "scrambling published leaderboards," "Length, not correctness, is doing much of the work") are rhetorical sharpenings of claims already made in the artifact, not new assertions — but if the paper's actual evidence is weaker than the abstract suggests (e.g., only three systems reorder, on one split, with one benchmark), reviewers may push back on the stronger phrasing. Consider softening if the body of the paper doesn't fully support the leaderboard-wide framing.
